sentences.base_language: Good morning. I need to see a doctor.
sentences.target_language: Buenos días. Necesito ver a un médico.
sentences.grammarNote: The 'personal a' is required before a specific person as a direct object: 'ver a un médico' (to see a doctor), 'llamar a mi hermano' (to call my brother), 'ayudar a los pacientes' (to help the patients). It has no equivalent in English.

sentences.base_language: My head hurts and I have a sore throat.
sentences.target_language: Me duele la cabeza y me duele la garganta.

sentences.grammarNote: Many medical symptoms use tener: tener fiebre (to have a fever), tener náuseas (to feel nauseous), tener dolor de cabeza (to have a headache), tener tos (to have a cough). Contrast with 'me duele' for specific pain: 'me duele la cabeza'.

sentences.base_language: Since yesterday. I feel very tired.
sentences.target_language: Desde ayer. Me siento muy cansado.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Desde' = since / from a point in time. 'Desde ayer' (since yesterday), 'desde las tres' (since three o'clock), 'desde hace dos días' (for two days). Compare: 'hace dos días que me duele' (my head has been hurting for two days).

sentences.base_language: Open your mouth and show me your throat.
sentences.target_language: Abra la boca y muéstreme la garganta.
sentences.grammarNote: Formal (usted) commands: abra (open), muéstreme (show me), respire (breathe), tome (take). Informal (tú): abre, muéstrame, respira, toma. Doctors typically use usted with patients.

sentences.target_language: Tiene una infección en la garganta.

sentences.base_language: Take this medicine and rest for two days.
sentences.target_language: Tome este medicamento y descanse dos días.
